platforms.name: Claude Code
platforms.tagline: Anthropic’s coding agent for terminal, desktop, web and IDE — 92 built-in commands.

platforms.name: Codex CLI
platforms.tagline: OpenAI’s coding agent for the terminal — subcommands, flags and interactive slash commands.

platforms.name: Cursor CLI
platforms.tagline: Cursor's terminal agent — runs as the »agent« binary in your terminal, in scripts and CI.

commands.name: /add-dir
commands.summary: Adds an additional working directory for file access in the current session.
commands.what:
Adds an additional working directory for file access in the current session. Most .claude/ configuration is not automatically loaded from the added directory.

You can later resume the session from the added directory with --continue or --resume.

whenGood.title: Briefly access a second codebase without switching sessions
whenGood.example: /add-dir ../shared-lib — look up a utility function from the shared lib while continuing work in the main project

whenGood.title: Pull in reference files from a neighboring folder while keeping the prompt cache intact
whenGood.example: /add-dir ../design-tokens — make design tokens available as reference for a styling task

whenBad.title: Wanting to permanently switch to another project with its own CLAUDE.md
whenBad.why: /add-dir does not automatically load the .claude/ configuration of the added directory
whenBad.alternative: use /cd instead — it picks up the target folder's CLAUDE.md and moves the session into its project storage

commands.name: /advisor
commands.summary: Turns the Advisor tool on or off, which brings in a second model for consultation at key moments in a task.
commands.what:
Turns the Advisor tool on or off, which brings in a second model for consultation at key moments in a task.

Accepts opus, sonnet, fable (from v2.1.170), or a full model ID. Without an argument, a picker opens. Requires Claude Code v2.1.98 or newer.

whenGood.title: Bring in a stronger model for a second opinion before a risky architecture decision
whenGood.example: /advisor opus — get a second opinion before implementing a database migration

whenGood.title: Cut costs in a long loop by combining a cheap executor with an advisor consulted only when needed
whenGood.example: /advisor sonnet — as advisor for an automation loop otherwise running on haiku

whenBad.title: Leaving it on for every trivial small task
whenBad.why: each advisory round costs extra tokens/time with no benefit for routine work
whenBad.alternative: open /advisor with no argument and turn it off, re-enable only at real decision points

commands.name: /agents
commands.summary: Manages subagent configurations.
commands.what:
Manages subagent configurations.

From v2.1.198, /agents just shows a hint to ask Claude directly to create/manage subagents, or to edit .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/ yourself. Up to v2.1.197, the command opened an interactive management interface.

whenGood.title: Ask Claude to create a new subagent configuration
whenGood.example: /agents, then: "create a subagent for database migrations for me"

whenGood.title: Review existing subagent definitions before adjusting them in the current project
whenGood.example: /agents to be pointed to .claude/agents/

whenBad.title: Expecting an interactive management UI
whenBad.why: since v2.1.198, /agents only shows a hint instead of an interactive UI
whenBad.alternative: edit .claude/agents/ or ~/.claude/agents/ directly in an editor

commands.name: /autofix-pr
commands.summary: Starts a Claude Code web session that watches the current branch's pull request and automatically pushes fixes for CI failures or reviewer comments.
commands.what:
Starts a Claude Code web session that watches the current branch's pull request and automatically pushes fixes for CI failures or reviewer comments.

Detects the open PR via 'gh pr view'; a prompt can be passed to give different instructions (e.g. only lint/type errors). Requires the gh CLI and access to Claude Code on the web.

whenGood.title: Let an open PR run unattended against CI failures
whenGood.example: /autofix-pr only fix lint and type errors — after pushing a PR, while working on something else

whenGood.title: Have reviewer comments automatically turned into fixes
whenGood.example: /autofix-pr address all review comments from the last review round

whenBad.title: Changes haven't been pushed as a PR yet
whenBad.why: the command detects the PR via 'gh pr view' and needs Claude Code on the web access — with no open PR it finds nothing
whenBad.alternative: commit/push normally and open a PR with gh pr create first, then run /autofix-pr

commands.name: /background
commands.summary: Detaches the current session from the terminal and lets it keep running as a background agent, freeing up the terminal.
commands.what:
Detaches the current session from the terminal and lets it keep running as a background agent, freeing up the terminal.

With a prompt argument, one final instruction is sent before detaching. Monitor via 'claude agents'. Alias: /bg.

whenGood.title: Free up the terminal while the session keeps running in the background
whenGood.example: /background

whenGood.title: Give one last instruction before detaching
whenGood.example: /background run the tests one more time before you go

whenBad.title: Want to watch the result synchronously
whenBad.why: /background detaches the session from the terminal right now - if you want to see the answer directly, detaching offers no benefit.
whenBad.alternative: just wait in the running session

whenBad.title: Side task with inherited context in parallel with the main session
whenBad.why: /background detaches the entire session, not just a sub-task.
whenBad.alternative: /fork

commands.name: /batch
commands.summary: Orchestrates large changes in parallel across the entire codebase: researches, splits the work into units, and has subagents implement them.
commands.what:
Orchestrates large changes in parallel across the entire codebase: researches, splits the work into units, and has subagents implement them.

[Skill] Breaks the work into 5-30 independent units and presents a plan; once approved, a background subagent starts per unit in an isolated git worktree, implements, tests, and opens a pull request. Requires a git repository.

whenGood.title: A large change that splits into many independent units across the whole codebase
whenGood.example: /batch rename the legacy HTTP client to the new SDK across all 15 services

whenGood.title: A migration where each unit should get its own PR and be tested in parallel
whenGood.example: /batch migrate every module from CommonJS to ESM

whenBad.title: A small task touching one or two files
whenBad.why: the planning phase plus isolated worktrees and per-unit PRs are pure overhead for a small task
whenBad.alternative: use a normal prompt or a single subagent instead of /batch

whenBad.title: No git repository present
whenBad.why: /batch requires a git repository for the isolated per-unit worktrees
whenBad.alternative: run 'git init' first, or make the change manually without /batch

commands.name: /branch
commands.summary: Creates a branch of the current conversation from this point, to try a different direction without losing the history so far.
commands.what:
Creates a branch of the current conversation from this point, to try a different direction without losing the history so far.

Automatically switches to the new branch; the original is preserved and reachable via /resume. For a side task on a background subagent instead of switching yourself: /fork.

whenGood.title: Try an alternative solution path from the current point
whenGood.example: /branch

whenGood.title: Explore a fundamentally different direction and keep working there yourself
whenGood.example: /branch try server-side instead of client-side rendering

whenBad.title: Side task should run in the background in parallel
whenBad.why: branch vs fork: branch switches you into the new branch yourself - for a side task with inherited context running in the background, /fork is meant for that.
whenBad.alternative: /fork

commands.name: /btw
commands.summary: Asks a quick side question without adding it to the main conversation.
commands.what: Asks a quick side question without adding it to the main conversation.

whenGood.title: Ask a quick side question without loading up the main context
whenGood.example: /btw what does the --frozen-lockfile flag do? — while mid-way through a feature session

whenGood.title: Clarify an unrelated question without branching the ongoing conversation
whenGood.example: /btw is there a Windows equivalent for this bash command?

whenBad.title: The answer will be needed later in the main context
whenBad.why: /btw does not add the question and answer to the main conversation
whenBad.alternative: ask as a normal message in the main chat; for a completely new topic use /clear for a fresh start instead

commands.name: /cd
commands.summary: Moves the session to a new working directory.
commands.what:
Moves the session to a new working directory.

The conversation's prompt cache is preserved: the new directory's CLAUDE.md is appended as a message instead of rebuilding the system prompt. The session is moved to the new directory's project storage, so --resume/--continue will find it there. Asks for trust the first time a directory is used. Can be restricted/disabled via 'Cd' permission rules. For access to an additional directory without moving: /add-dir. Requires Claude Code v2.1.169+.

whenGood.title: Permanently move to the next project whose CLAUDE.md should apply from now on
whenGood.example: /cd ../other-project — continue the session fully in the new project

whenGood.title: Store the session in the target folder's project storage so --resume finds it there
whenGood.example: /cd ~/work/client-b before starting the next task

whenBad.title: Just briefly need a file from a neighboring folder while the main project stays active
whenBad.why: /cd fully switches the session instead of just adding access
whenBad.alternative: use /add-dir to access the extra directory without moving

commands.name: /chrome
commands.summary: Opens the settings for 'Claude in Chrome'.
commands.what: Opens the settings for 'Claude in Chrome'.

whenGood.title: Set up the Claude in Chrome extension before letting Claude take actions in the browser
whenGood.example: /chrome — enable the extension/check permissions before a browser automation task

whenGood.title: Review existing Chrome integration settings
whenGood.example: /chrome to check which sites/permissions the extension currently has

whenBad.title: Just wanting to read or search a webpage, no browser control needed
whenBad.why: /chrome only opens the Chrome extension's settings, not a search function
whenBad.alternative: use /web or /web-search for pure reading/searching the web

commands.name: /claude-api
commands.summary: Loads Claude API reference material for the project language (Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, cURL) as well as Managed Agents reference.
commands.what:
Loads Claude API reference material for the project language (Python, TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, C#, PHP, cURL) as well as Managed Agents reference.

[Skill] Covers tool use, streaming, batches, structured outputs, and common pitfalls. Also triggers automatically on imports of 'anthropic'/'@anthropic-ai/sdk'. The 'migrate' subcommand updates existing API code to a newer model (model IDs, thinking configuration, etc.). The 'managed-agents-onboard' subcommand walks interactively through creating a new Managed Agent.

whenGood.title: Load reference material before writing your own code against the Claude API
whenGood.example: /claude-api — before building a custom streaming handler for the Messages API

whenGood.title: Migrate existing API code to a newer model
whenGood.example: /claude-api migrate — update model IDs and thinking configuration in existing code

whenBad.title: The question is only about operating the Claude Code CLI itself, not your own API code
whenBad.why: /claude-api loads API reference material (Python/TS/Java/...), not CLI documentation
whenBad.alternative: use the claude-code-guide agent or the official Claude Code docs for questions about the CLI itself

commands.name: /clear
commands.summary: Starts a new conversation with an empty context; the previous one remains available via /resume.
commands.what:
Starts a new conversation with an empty context; the previous one remains available via /resume.

With a name, the previous conversation is labeled accordingly in the /resume picker. To just free up context in an ongoing conversation, use /compact instead. Aliases: /reset, /new.

whenGood.title: Start a new conversation on a completely different topic
whenGood.example: /clear

whenGood.title: Set aside the old conversation under a name
whenGood.example: /clear bugfix-session

whenBad.title: Just freeing up context during ongoing work
whenBad.why: /clear starts a brand-new conversation with empty context - per the docs, /compact is meant for simply freeing up context during ongoing work.
whenBad.alternative: /compact

commands.name: /code-review
commands.summary: Reviews the current diff for correctness bugs as well as reuse, simplification, and efficiency improvements.
commands.what:
Reviews the current diff for correctness bugs as well as reuse, simplification, and efficiency improvements.

[Skill] Effort levels: low, medium, high, xhigh, max, ultra. --fix applies findings to the working tree, --comment posts them as inline comments on a GitHub PR, 'ultra' starts a deep cloud review. From v2.1.154, /simplify handles the pure cleanup review without bug hunting. Details: code.claude.com/docs/en/code-review.

whenGood.title: Check a diff for bugs plus reuse/efficiency improvements
whenGood.example: /code-review high

whenGood.title: Deep cloud review for a critical diff
whenGood.example: /code-review ultra

whenBad.title: Pure cleanup review without bug-hunting
whenBad.why: Per the docs, since v2.1.154 /simplify handles that, cleaning up directly without hunting for correctness bugs.
whenBad.alternative: /simplify

whenBad.title: Quick single pass over a GitHub PR
whenBad.why: For a quick, read-only single pass by PR number, the lighter-weight command is meant for that.
whenBad.alternative: /review <PR number>

commands.name: /color
commands.summary: Sets the prompt bar color for the current session.
commands.what:
Sets the prompt bar color for the current session.

Colors: red, blue, green, yellow, purple, orange, pink, cyan. 'default' resets it, no argument picks a random color. Synced to claude.ai/code under Remote Control. Also usable non-interactively (-p), requires v2.1.205+.

whenGood.title: Make several parallel sessions visually distinguishable
whenGood.example: /color blue in session A, /color green in session B

whenGood.title: Quickly set a random color for the current session
whenGood.example: /color with no argument

whenBad.title: Expecting the color to persist for all future sessions
whenBad.why: /color only sets the color for the current session
whenBad.alternative: set it again at the start of each session, or configure /statusline for a persistently visible distinguishing marker

commands.name: /compact
commands.summary: Summarizes the conversation so far to free up context.
commands.what:
Summarizes the conversation so far to free up context.

Optionally, a focus instruction can be given for the summary.

whenGood.title: Free up context during ongoing work on the same topic
whenGood.example: /compact

whenGood.title: Summarize with a focus instruction
whenGood.example: /compact focus: open TODOs and next steps

whenBad.title: Complete topic change
whenBad.why: /compact summarizes the conversation so far and keeps the thread going - for a complete topic change that drags along unnecessary baggage.
whenBad.alternative: /clear

commands.name: /config
commands.summary: Opens the settings interface for theme, model, output style, and other preferences.
commands.what:
Opens the settings interface for theme, model, output style, and other preferences.

From v2.1.181 it can be set directly with key=value (e.g. /config thinking=false); from v2.1.182 named shortcuts are available (/config theme=dark, /config model=sonnet). key=value also works non-interactively (-p) and via Remote Control. '/config --help' lists all keys. Alias: /settings.

whenGood.title: Set a single known value directly via command
whenGood.example: /config theme=dark or /config model=sonnet

whenGood.title: Browse interactively through all settings when unsure what to change
whenGood.example: /config with no argument opens the settings UI

whenBad.title: Expecting the interactive dialog to work in a non-interactive script
whenBad.why: the interactive UI isn't suited for automated/non-interactive (-p) runs
whenBad.alternative: use /config key=value directly — it also works non-interactive and via Remote Control

commands.name: /context
commands.summary: Visualizes current context usage as a colored grid with optimization suggestions.
commands.what:
Visualizes current context usage as a colored grid with optimization suggestions.

Shows hints about context-heavy tools, memory bloat, and capacity warnings. Argument 'all' expands the detailed breakdown.

whenGood.title: Check how much context is still free before continuing a long session
whenGood.example: /context all for the detailed breakdown

whenGood.title: Find the cause of unusually high context usage
whenGood.example: /context to identify context-heavy tools or bloated memory

whenBad.title: Actually wanting to free up context
whenBad.why: /context only displays usage, it doesn't change the context itself
whenBad.alternative: use /compact to summarize the conversation and free up context

commands.name: /copy
commands.summary: Copies Claude's last response to the clipboard.
commands.what:
Copies Claude's last response to the clipboard.

With a number N, the N-th-to-last response is copied. For code blocks, an interactive picker appears; key 'w' writes to a file (useful over SSH).

whenGood.title: Grab the last response directly into the clipboard
whenGood.example: /copy after a response containing a finished code snippet

whenGood.title: Write the response to a file instead, over SSH without a working clipboard
whenGood.example: /copy, then press 'w' to export to a file

whenBad.title: Wanting to copy an older response, not the last one
whenBad.why: without a number argument, /copy always copies only the last response
whenBad.alternative: use /copy 3 for the third-to-last response, or /export for the whole conversation

commands.name: /cost
commands.summary: Alias for /usage.
commands.what: Alias for /usage.

whenGood.title: Quickly check API cost/token usage without remembering the name /usage
whenGood.example: /cost

whenBad.title: Expecting extra functionality beyond /usage
whenBad.why: /cost is just an alias, not a separate command with different behavior
whenBad.alternative: use /usage directly, or /stats for the stats tab

commands.name: /dataviz
commands.summary: Provides design help for charts, graphs, and dashboards.
commands.what:
Provides design help for charts, graphs, and dashboards.

[Skill] Picks a fitting chart form, assigns color by role, validates the palette for colorblind safety/contrast via script. Requires v2.1.198+.

whenGood.title: Get the right chart form and a colorblind-safe palette before building a dashboard
whenGood.example: /dataviz before building a KPI dashboard with several chart types

whenGood.title: Have an existing visualization's color choices checked for contrast/colorblind safety
whenGood.example: /dataviz to validate the color mapping of an existing chart

whenBad.title: Pure data analysis with no visualization to follow
whenBad.why: /dataviz helps with chart design, not with the data analysis itself
whenBad.alternative: use a normal analysis prompt, bring in /dataviz only once results are being shown as a chart

commands.name: /debug
commands.summary: Enables debug logging for the current session and helps with troubleshooting via the session debug log.
commands.what:
Enables debug logging for the current session and helps with troubleshooting via the session debug log.

[Skill] Debug logging is off by default (unless started with 'claude --debug'); running /debug mid-session captures logs only from that point on. Optionally, a problem description can be given to focus it.

whenGood.title: Start capturing debug logs from now on for a reproducible problem
whenGood.example: /debug MCP server keeps disconnecting — right before triggering the problem again

whenGood.title: Systematically enable logging before a hard-to-trace bug hunt
whenGood.example: /debug to capture tool calls and errors in the session debug log

whenBad.title: The error already happened earlier
whenBad.why: /debug only captures logs from the moment it's activated, earlier events are missing
whenBad.alternative: restart the session with 'claude --debug' to log from the very start

commands.name: /deep-research
commands.summary: Fans out web searches for a question, fetches and cross-checks sources, and produces a cited report.
commands.what:
Fans out web searches for a question, fetches and cross-checks sources, and produces a cited report.

[Workflow] Runs as a bundled dynamic workflow, distributes work across many subagents, runs in the background.

whenGood.title: A thorough, multi-source research task with citations on an open question
whenGood.example: /deep-research what are current best practices for LLM prompt caching in production?

whenGood.title: Let research run in the background while working on something else
whenGood.example: /deep-research compare pricing models of major vector database providers

whenBad.title: A quick single lookup of a concrete fact
whenBad.why: fanning out across many subagents is unnecessary overhead for one quick lookup
whenBad.alternative: use /web-search for the quick single lookup

commands.name: /design-login
commands.summary: Authorizes access to the design system for /design-sync via the claude.ai account.
commands.what: Authorizes access to the design system for /design-sync via the claude.ai account.

whenGood.title: One-time authorization before first using /design-sync
whenGood.example: /design-login before the first sync of your own React design system

whenBad.title: Expecting the command to already sync designs
whenBad.why: /design-login only authorizes access via the claude.ai account, it doesn't sync anything itself
whenBad.alternative: run /design-sync afterwards for the actual sync

commands.name: /design-sync
commands.summary: Converts your repo's React design system and uploads it to Claude Design, so designs it creates use real components.
commands.what:
Converts your repo's React design system and uploads it to Claude Design, so designs it creates use real components.

[Skill] The initial sync verifies every component and can take hours on large repos. Not available on Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry.

whenGood.title: Upload your own React component system so generated designs use real project components
whenGood.example: set up /design-sync once after /design-login

whenGood.title: Re-sync after larger component changes
whenGood.example: run /design-sync again after reworking the button and card component set

whenBad.title: Using it on Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry
whenBad.why: /design-sync is not available on these platforms
whenBad.alternative: use a standard Anthropic account/Claude Code access, or work with generic components without design-sync

whenBad.title: Starting the first sync right before a deadline
whenBad.why: the first sync verifies every component and can take hours on large repos
whenBad.alternative: schedule it well ahead of time instead of right before a deadline

commands.name: /desktop
commands.summary: Continues the current session in the Claude Code desktop app.
commands.what:
Continues the current session in the Claude Code desktop app.

Requires macOS or Windows plus a Claude subscription. Alias: /app.

whenGood.title: Seamlessly carry a running terminal session over to the desktop app
whenGood.example: /desktop mid-session, to keep working on a bigger screen

whenGood.title: Switch from CLI to a GUI-centered workflow without losing context
whenGood.example: /desktop when moving from a laptop on the go to a desktop machine

whenBad.title: Linux without a Claude subscription
whenBad.why: /desktop requires macOS or Windows plus a Claude subscription
whenBad.alternative: stay in the terminal session, or use /remote-control to control the session from claude.ai instead

commands.name: /diff
commands.summary: Opens an interactive diff viewer for uncommitted changes and turn-by-turn diffs.
commands.what:
Opens an interactive diff viewer for uncommitted changes and turn-by-turn diffs.

Left/right switches between git diff and Claude turns, up/down scrolls through files. From v2.1.198, the viewer automatically refreshes on external git changes.

whenGood.title: Go through uncommitted changes file by file before committing
whenGood.example: /diff after several edits, to review each changed file

whenGood.title: Trace which change originated in which conversation turn
whenGood.example: /diff, then switch between git diff and Claude turns with left/right

whenBad.title: Wanting to compare changes across multiple commits/branches
whenBad.why: /diff only shows uncommitted changes and turn-by-turn diffs, not commit history
whenBad.alternative: use a normal 'git diff <ref1> <ref2>' in the terminal for cross-commit comparisons

commands.name: /doctor
commands.summary: Runs a setup check that diagnoses and can fix problems.
commands.what:
Runs a setup check that diagnoses and can fix problems.

[Skill] Checks the installation (duplicates, PATH, broken settings), finds unused skills/MCP servers/plugins versus their context cost, deduplicates CLAUDE.md, flags slow hooks, checks versions. Reports findings first, asks before making changes. 'claude doctor' in the terminal is read-only. Alias: /checkup. Before v2.1.205 it was a read-only screen.

whenGood.title: Setup check after an update or suspected broken configuration
whenGood.example: /doctor

whenGood.title: Find unused skills/MCP servers vs. context cost and deduplicate CLAUDE.md
whenGood.example: /doctor

whenBad.title: No setup problem, just memory diagnostics under high memory usage
whenBad.why: /doctor checks the installation and setup, not the JavaScript heap.
whenBad.alternative: /heapdump

whenBad.title: Just want to see current session context usage
whenBad.why: /doctor checks the setup, not how much of the current context window is used.
whenBad.alternative: /context

commands.name: /effort
commands.summary: Sets the model's effort level (reasoning depth).
commands.what:
Sets the model's effort level (reasoning depth).

Values: low, medium, high, xhigh, max, ultracode — depending on the model; max/ultracode only for the current session. ultracode = xhigh reasoning plus automatic workflow orchestration. 'auto' = model default. Without an argument, an interactive slider opens. Takes effect immediately. From v2.1.205 also non-interactive (-p).

whenGood.title: Raise thinking effort before a tricky task
whenGood.example: /effort high

whenGood.title: Pick a level interactively via slider
whenGood.example: /effort

whenBad.title: Fundamentally need a different model
whenBad.why: /effort only changes the thinking-effort level of the current model, not its capabilities.
whenBad.alternative: /model

commands.name: /exit
commands.summary: Exits the CLI.
commands.what:
Exits the CLI.

In an attached background session, only the connection is closed; the session keeps running. Alias: /quit.

whenGood.title: Cleanly end the session once the task is done
whenGood.example: /exit after finishing the work

whenBad.title: Actually wanting to stop an attached background session
whenBad.why: in an attached background session, /exit only disconnects — the session keeps running
whenBad.alternative: manage/stop the background session via 'claude agents' instead of just /exit

commands.name: /export
commands.summary: Exports the current conversation as plain text.
commands.what:
Exports the current conversation as plain text.

With a filename, it goes straight to a file; without one, a dialog offers clipboard or file.

whenGood.title: Save or share the whole conversation as text
whenGood.example: /export session-notes.txt

whenGood.title: Decide between clipboard and file via the dialog
whenGood.example: /export with no argument opens the choice

whenBad.title: Only wanting to copy the last response
whenBad.why: /export exports the entire conversation, not a single response
whenBad.alternative: use /copy for just the last (or, with a number, the Nth-last) response

commands.name: /fast
commands.summary: Turns Fast mode on or off.
commands.what:
Turns Fast mode on or off.

Non-interactive (-p) only in sessions started with fastMode in --settings; the toggle only applies to the session. Requires v2.1.205+.

whenGood.title: Prioritize speed over depth for a series of simple, repetitive edits
whenGood.example: /fast for a run of similar, simple changes

whenGood.title: Turn fast mode back off once more complex tasks come up
whenGood.example: call /fast again to toggle it back

whenBad.title: Non-interactive use without fastMode in the start settings
whenBad.why: non-interactive (-p) toggling of /fast only works in sessions started with fastMode set in --settings
whenBad.alternative: pass fastMode directly in --settings when starting the session

commands.name: /feedback
commands.summary: Sends feedback, reports a bug, or shares the conversation.
commands.what:
Sends feedback, reports a bug, or shares the conversation.

Aliases: /bug, /share.

whenGood.title: Report a bug in Claude Code itself
whenGood.example: /feedback or /bug after hitting a reproducible tool error

whenGood.title: Share a conversation with someone
whenGood.example: /share to pass along a helpful conversation

whenBad.title: Wanting to report a bug in your own project code
whenBad.why: /feedback addresses issues with Claude Code itself, not your own application
whenBad.alternative: use a normal bug-fix prompt or file an issue in your own repo

commands.name: /fewer-permission-prompts
commands.summary: Scans your own transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls and adds a prioritized allowlist to the project-wide .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
commands.what:
Scans your own transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls and adds a prioritized allowlist to the project-wide .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

[Skill]

whenGood.title: Auto-allow recurring, harmless read-only commands after a few sessions
whenGood.example: /fewer-permission-prompts after several sessions full of repeated permission prompts for git status, ls, etc.

whenGood.title: Generate a sensible allowlist baseline for team onboarding
whenGood.example: /fewer-permission-prompts to pre-populate .claude/settings.json with common read-only calls

whenBad.title: Wanting to blanket-allow write/risky commands too, without prompts
whenBad.why: the skill specifically scans for common read-only Bash/MCP calls, not write commands
whenBad.alternative: keep allowing write/risky commands manually and deliberately in .claude/settings.json (e.g. via the update-config skill)

commands.name: /focus
commands.summary: Toggles Focus view: only the last prompt, a one-line tool summary with edit diffstats, and the final answer.
commands.what:
Toggles Focus view: only the last prompt, a one-line tool summary with edit diffstats, and the final answer.

From v2.1.198, the summary also counts started subagents. The choice persists across sessions ('viewMode' setting). Full-screen rendering only.

whenGood.title: Get an overview of long multi-tool turns
whenGood.example: Before a task with many file edits, turn on /focus to see only the last prompt, a one-line tool-call summary with diffstats, and the final response instead of every intermediate message.

whenGood.title: Screen-sharing or demoing without tool noise
whenGood.example: Enable /focus before a presentation so viewers see only the key steps instead of every single tool call.

whenBad.title: Debugging a failed tool call
whenBad.why: Focus collapses tool calls into one-liners — details like a stack trace or the full diff content are hidden.
whenBad.alternative: Run /focus again to turn it off and see the full turn output.

commands.name: /fork
commands.summary: Starts a background subagent that inherits the full conversation and continues working on the given directive, while the main session keeps running.
commands.what:
Starts a background subagent that inherits the full conversation and continues working on the given directive, while the main session keeps running.

The result returns to the conversation once finished. To switch yourself into a copy instead: /branch. Before v2.1.161, /fork was an alias for /branch.

whenGood.title: Handle a side task in the background while the main session keeps going
whenGood.example: /fork research all open TODOs in the repo

whenGood.title: Try a risky change in parallel with full context
whenGood.example: /fork try the approach with a Redis cache

whenBad.title: Want to switch direction yourself
whenBad.why: branch vs fork: fork starts a background subagent while you stay in the main session - for switching yourself into a new branch, /branch is meant for that.
whenBad.alternative: /branch

commands.name: /goal
commands.summary: Sets a goal: Claude keeps working across multiple turns until the condition is met.
commands.what:
Sets a goal: Claude keeps working across multiple turns until the condition is met.

Without an argument, the command shows the current or most recently achieved goal. clear/stop/off/reset/none/cancel remove an active goal early.

whenGood.title: Multi-step task with a clear completion condition
whenGood.example: /goal get all tests passing

whenGood.title: Stop an active goal early
whenGood.example: /goal stop

whenBad.title: Recurring standing task
whenBad.why: /goal is meant for a goal with a completion condition, not for a check that should keep recurring across sessions.
whenBad.alternative: /loop (e.g. /loop 30m check for new support tickets)

whenBad.title: Exploring a fundamentally different direction
whenBad.why: Setting a new goal only changes the completion condition, not the approach taken so far.
whenBad.alternative: /branch, to branch off a different approach from this point

commands.name: /heapdump
commands.summary: Writes a JavaScript heap snapshot and a memory breakdown to diagnose high memory usage.
commands.what:
Writes a JavaScript heap snapshot and a memory breakdown to diagnose high memory usage.

Target: ~/Desktop; on Linux without a Desktop folder, the home directory.

whenGood.title: Claude Code is using unusually high memory
whenGood.example: The terminal gets noticeably slow and Task Manager shows high RAM usage → run /heapdump; the snapshot lands on ~/Desktop (or the home directory on Linux without a Desktop folder) for analysis.

whenGood.title: Document memory usage for a bug report
whenGood.example: Before reporting a suspected memory leak, run /heapdump and attach the generated snapshot file to the bug report.

whenBad.title: The conversation's context window is full
whenBad.why: heapdump measures the process's JavaScript memory usage, not the conversation's token usage.
whenBad.alternative: Use /context to visualize context usage as a grid.

whenBad.title: General installation or setup problems
whenBad.why: heapdump only produces a memory snapshot, not a diagnosis of installation or configuration issues.
whenBad.alternative: Use /doctor for a setup check.

commands.name: /help
commands.summary: Shows help and available commands.
commands.what: Shows help and available commands.

whenGood.title: Getting first orientation in Claude Code
whenGood.example: Right after installing, type /help to see the list of available commands.

whenGood.title: Looking up a forgotten command name
whenGood.example: You only vaguely remember a command name → /help shows the full overview to look it up.

whenBad.title: Clarifying detailed questions about one specific feature
whenBad.why: /help only gives a short overview of commands, not a detailed explanation of individual features.
whenBad.alternative: Consult the official docs (code.claude.com/docs) or ask directly.

commands.name: /hooks
commands.summary: Shows hook configurations for tool events.
commands.what: Shows hook configurations for tool events.

whenGood.title: Check which hooks currently apply to tool events
whenGood.example: Before adding a new pre-tool hook, run /hooks first to avoid conflicts with existing hook configurations.

whenGood.title: Figure out why a tool call was altered or blocked
whenGood.example: After a Bash call gets unexpectedly denied, open /hooks to see whether an existing hook configuration is responsible.

whenBad.title: Editing or reconfiguring hook rules
whenBad.why: /hooks only displays the configurations, it is not an editing interface.
whenBad.alternative: Edit the hook configuration directly in settings.json, or use the update-config skill.

commands.name: /ide
commands.summary: Manages IDE integrations and shows their status.
commands.what: Manages IDE integrations and shows their status.

whenGood.title: Verify the IDE connection
whenGood.example: After opening VS Code alongside the terminal, run /ide to confirm Claude Code is connected.

whenGood.title: Check the status of multiple open IDE integrations
whenGood.example: With several editor windows open, use /ide to see which integration is currently active.

whenBad.title: Installing the IDE extension for the first time
whenBad.why: /ide only manages existing integrations and shows their status — it does not install a new editor extension.
whenBad.alternative: Install the matching Claude Code extension directly from the editor's marketplace (e.g. the VS Code Marketplace).

commands.name: /init
commands.summary: Initializes the project with a CLAUDE.md guide.
commands.what:
Initializes the project with a CLAUDE.md guide.

With CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=1, an interactive flow runs, including skills, hooks, and personal memory files.

whenGood.title: Prepare a new repository for Claude Code
whenGood.example: In a freshly cloned project, run /init so Claude creates an initial CLAUDE.md with project context.

whenGood.title: Full setup including skills, hooks, and memory
whenGood.example: With the environment variable CLAUDE_CODE_NEW_INIT=1 set, start /init to interactively set up skills, hooks, and personal memory files as well.

whenBad.title: A well-maintained, project-specific CLAUDE.md already exists
whenBad.why: Running /init again can overwrite or needlessly alter existing, carefully curated project docs.
whenBad.alternative: Edit CLAUDE.md manually and selectively instead of re-initializing the project.

commands.name: /insights
commands.summary: Generates a report that analyzes your own Claude Code sessions, including project areas, interaction patterns, and friction points.
commands.what: Generates a report that analyzes your own Claude Code sessions, including project areas, interaction patterns, and friction points.

whenGood.title: Reflect on your own Claude Code usage
whenGood.example: After an intense project week, run /insights to see which project areas got the most work.

whenGood.title: Spot recurring friction points in your workflow
whenGood.example: Use /insights to surface patterns like frequent clarifying questions or interaction issues across sessions.

whenBad.title: Getting an instant short summary of the current session
whenBad.why: /insights analyzes sessions after the fact for patterns and friction points, it doesn't give a quick one-sentence summary of the ongoing conversation.
whenBad.alternative: Use /recap for a one-line summary of the current session.

commands.name: /install-github-app
commands.summary: Installs the Claude GitHub App for a repository, optionally including GitHub Actions workflows and secrets.
commands.what:
Installs the Claude GitHub App for a repository, optionally including GitHub Actions workflows and secrets.

Walks you through repo selection and configuration.

whenGood.title: Set up automated PR checks or CI integration
whenGood.example: Run /install-github-app in a team repository to enable Claude for automated PR workflows via GitHub Actions.

whenGood.title: Prepare repo access for later Claude automations
whenGood.example: For a new project, walk through /install-github-app including repo selection and secrets so workflows like /autofix-pr can work.

whenBad.title: Working purely locally, without GitHub integration
whenBad.why: /install-github-app sets up a connection to GitHub including optional Actions and secrets — unnecessary overhead if PRs or CI will never run through GitHub.
whenBad.alternative: Keep working locally with Git and skip the GitHub app.

commands.name: /install-slack-app
commands.summary: Installs the Claude Slack app.
commands.what:
Installs the Claude Slack app.

Opens the browser to complete the OAuth flow.

whenGood.title: Make Claude usable in a team Slack workspace
whenGood.example: Run /install-slack-app to enable Claude Tag in the company workspace for colleagues.

whenGood.title: Start requests directly from a Slack channel
whenGood.example: After installation, mention Claude in a Slack channel to kick off a request directly from chat.

whenBad.title: No Slack in use, or no admin rights in the workspace
whenBad.why: Installation requires an OAuth flow in the browser and appropriate workspace permissions — without Slack usage it provides no benefit.
whenBad.alternative: Use Claude Code directly in the terminal or IDE.

commands.name: /keybindings
commands.summary: Opens the keybindings file.
commands.what: Opens the keybindings file.

whenGood.title: Customize keyboard shortcuts
whenGood.example: Open /keybindings to change a shortcut that conflicts with another application.

whenBad.title: Just quickly checking which shortcuts exist, without changing anything
whenBad.why: /keybindings opens the editable file directly rather than a plain overview.
whenBad.alternative: Consult the keyboard shortcuts documentation instead of opening the file.

commands.name: /login
commands.summary: Signs in to your Anthropic account.
commands.what: Signs in to your Anthropic account.

whenGood.title: First-time sign-in or switching accounts
whenGood.example: On a new machine, run /login to connect with the Anthropic account.

whenBad.title: Just checking whether you're already logged in
whenBad.why: /login starts the sign-in flow directly, it doesn't show a plain status view.
whenBad.alternative: Use /status to see account and connection info without starting a new login flow.

commands.name: /logout
commands.summary: Signs out of your Anthropic account.
commands.what: Signs out of your Anthropic account.

whenGood.title: Cleanly disconnect an account from a device
whenGood.example: On a shared machine, run /logout after finishing work to sign out of your own Anthropic account.

whenBad.title: Just briefly checking whether, and with which account, you're logged in
whenBad.why: /logout ends the session immediately instead of just displaying information.
whenBad.alternative: Use /status to view account info without signing out.

commands.name: /loop
commands.summary: Runs a prompt repeatedly for as long as the session stays open.
commands.what:
Runs a prompt repeatedly for as long as the session stays open.

[Skill] Without an interval, Claude paces itself. Without a prompt, an autonomous maintenance check runs, or a prompt from .claude/loop.md. Example: '/loop 5m check if the deploy finished'. Docs: code.claude.com/docs/en/scheduled-tasks. Alias: /proactive.

whenGood.title: Recurring check on a fixed interval
whenGood.example: /loop 5m check if the deploy finished

whenGood.title: Autonomous maintenance check without a fixed prompt
whenGood.example: /loop

whenBad.title: One-off goal with an end condition
whenBad.why: /loop repeats a prompt as long as the session stays open - for a task that should finish once and stop, that's the wrong mechanism.
whenBad.alternative: /goal

whenBad.title: Schedule-based, beyond a single session
whenBad.why: /loop only runs while the current session is open, not on managed cloud infrastructure across sessions/days.
whenBad.alternative: /schedule

commands.name: /mcp
commands.summary: Manages MCP server connections and OAuth authentication.
commands.what:
Manages MCP server connections and OAuth authentication.

Without an argument, an interactive server list appears. 'reconnect <server>', 'enable'/'disable' with a name or 'all'. From v2.1.205 also non-interactive (-p) with a text summary.

whenGood.title: Fix the connection status of an MCP server
whenGood.example: An MCP server stops responding → run /mcp reconnect <server> to rebuild the connection.

whenGood.title: Selectively disable individual MCP servers
whenGood.example: Use /mcp disable <server> to temporarily turn off a rarely used server and reduce context/tool footprint.

whenBad.title: Setting up a brand-new MCP server for the first time
whenBad.why: /mcp only manages existing connections (reconnect, enable, disable) and OAuth authentication, it does not create a new server configuration.
whenBad.alternative: Add the server to the MCP configuration (project or user level) first, then use /mcp to manage it.

commands.name: /memory
commands.summary: Edits CLAUDE.md memory files, toggles auto-memory on/off, and shows auto-memory entries.
commands.what: Edits CLAUDE.md memory files, toggles auto-memory on/off, and shows auto-memory entries.

whenGood.title: Capture a permanent rule for all future sessions
whenGood.example: Use /memory to write a standing instruction like 'always use TypeScript strict mode' into CLAUDE.md instead of repeating it every time.

whenGood.title: Review auto-memory entries
whenGood.example: Run /memory to see which rules Claude has automatically saved as auto-memory from earlier sessions.

whenBad.title: An instruction only applies to the current task
whenBad.why: /memory writes to CLAUDE.md, which persists beyond the session — unnecessary for a one-off instruction and it clutters the file.
whenBad.alternative: Give the instruction directly in the current session's prompt without saving it to CLAUDE.md.

commands.name: /mobile
commands.summary: Shows a QR code to download the Claude mobile app.
commands.what:
Shows a QR code to download the Claude mobile app.

Aliases: /ios, /android.

whenGood.title: Install the Claude mobile app
whenGood.example: Run /mobile to scan the QR code and download the app to your phone.

whenBad.title: Continuing to steer a running terminal session on the go, without reinstalling the app
whenBad.why: /mobile only shows a QR code for installing the app, it doesn't hand off a running session.
whenBad.alternative: Enable /remote-control to steer the session remotely from claude.ai or the app.

commands.name: /model
commands.summary: Switches the AI model and saves it as the default for new sessions.
commands.what:
Switches the AI model and saves it as the default for new sessions.

Left/right arrow keys also adjust effort on supported models. Key 's' switches only for the current session. With existing history, a confirmation prompt appears (full history is reprocessed without the cache). From v2.1.205 also non-interactive (-p) with a model argument.

whenGood.title: Interactively switch the default model for new sessions
whenGood.example: /model

whenGood.title: Set a different model for just the current session
whenGood.example: /model (then press 's')

whenBad.title: Just need more thinking, not a different model
whenBad.why: effort vs model: switching models with existing history also triggers a confirmation prompt, because the full history is reprocessed without cache - unnecessary for pure thinking depth.
whenBad.alternative: /effort

commands.name: /passes
commands.summary: Shares a free week of Claude Code with friends.
commands.what:
Shares a free week of Claude Code with friends.

Only visible if the account is eligible.

whenGood.title: Share a free week of Claude Code with friends
whenGood.example: Run /passes (if the account is eligible) to give away a free week.

whenBad.title: Managing your own subscription or checking account status
whenBad.why: /passes is only for sharing free weeks and only visible if the account is eligible — it has nothing to do with managing your own plan.
whenBad.alternative: Use /upgrade or /status to view your own subscription or account status.

commands.name: /permissions
commands.summary: Manages allow, ask, and deny rules for tool permissions.
commands.what:
Manages allow, ask, and deny rules for tool permissions.

Interactive dialog: rules by scope, add/remove, working directories, recent auto-mode denials. Alias: /allowed-tools.

whenGood.title: Add a targeted allow or deny rule for a tool
whenGood.example: Open /permissions to add a deny rule for a specific Bash command in the interactive dialog.

whenGood.title: Review recently denied auto mode actions
whenGood.example: Run /permissions to see which tool calls were recently denied in auto mode, then adjust a rule accordingly.

whenBad.title: Bundling many recurring read-only prompts automatically
whenBad.why: /permissions edits rules one at a time through an interactive dialog — cumbersome for an allowlist generated automatically from the transcript.
whenBad.alternative: Use /fewer-permission-prompts, which builds a prioritized allowlist from your own transcripts.

commands.name: /plan
commands.summary: Switches directly from the prompt into Plan mode.
commands.what:
Switches directly from the prompt into Plan mode.

With an optional description, Plan mode starts immediately with that task, e.g. '/plan fix the auth bug'.

whenGood.title: Start a task directly in plan mode with a description
whenGood.example: /plan fix the auth bug

whenGood.title: Switch to plan mode from a running prompt
whenGood.example: /plan

whenBad.title: Trivial one-step change
whenBad.why: /plan switches mode specifically for a planning phase - for a clear, small change that's an unnecessary detour.
whenBad.alternative: just ask normally, without /plan

whenBad.title: Already mid-implementation with a plan in place
whenBad.why: /plan switches into plan mode again instead of continuing implementation.
whenBad.alternative: just keep working normally, without switching mode

commands.name: /plugin
commands.summary: Manages Claude Code plugins.
commands.what:
Manages Claude Code plugins.

Without an argument, a plugin menu opens. Subcommands list/install/enable/disable act directly.

whenGood.title: Install a new plugin directly
whenGood.example: Run /plugin install <name> to add a plugin without going through the menu.

whenGood.title: See an overview of all installed plugins
whenGood.example: Run /plugin with no argument to see in the plugin menu which plugins are active or inactive.

whenBad.title: Just reloading already-active plugins after a file change
whenBad.why: /plugin is for management (listing, installing, enabling), not for reloading already-active plugins after changes.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reload-plugins to apply changes without restarting.

commands.name: /powerup
commands.summary: Shows Claude Code features through short interactive lessons with animated demos.
commands.what: Shows Claude Code features through short interactive lessons with animated demos.

whenGood.title: Discover new Claude Code features playfully
whenGood.example: After an update, start /powerup to discover new features through short interactive lessons with demos.

whenGood.title: Onboard newcomers to Claude Code features
whenGood.example: Have a new team member go through /powerup instead of reading the entire documentation.

whenBad.title: Looking up a specific, already-known feature
whenBad.why: /powerup shows curated lessons, not a searchable reference of individual commands.
whenBad.alternative: Use /help for the command overview, or consult the docs directly.

commands.name: /privacy-settings
commands.summary: Shows and changes your privacy settings.
commands.what:
Shows and changes your privacy settings.

Only for Pro and Max plan subscribers.

whenGood.title: Check your own privacy settings
whenGood.example: Open /privacy-settings to see which privacy options are currently set.

whenGood.title: Adjust privacy settings to match internal requirements
whenGood.example: As a Pro or Max user, go through /privacy-settings to change settings to fit internal policy.

whenBad.title: Free-plan account without a Pro or Max subscription
whenBad.why: According to the docs, /privacy-settings is only visible to Pro and Max plan subscribers.
whenBad.alternative: Upgrade to Pro/Max first (/upgrade), or check general account settings via /status.

commands.name: /radio
commands.summary: Opens 'Claude FM' lo-fi radio in the browser.
commands.what:
Opens 'Claude FM' lo-fi radio in the browser.

Prints the stream URL if no browser is available. Not on Bedrock/Vertex/Foundry.

whenGood.title: Listen to lo-fi music in the background while coding
whenGood.example: Run /radio to open 'Claude FM' in the browser.

whenBad.title: Working on Bedrock, Vertex, or Foundry
whenBad.why: According to the docs, /radio is not available on these platforms.
whenBad.alternative: Use a separate music app or player alongside Claude Code.

commands.name: /recap
commands.summary: Generates a one-line summary of the current session on demand.
commands.what:
Generates a one-line summary of the current session on demand.

For automatic recaps after being away, see the 'Session recap' docs.

whenGood.title: Quickly get back up to speed after a break
whenGood.example: After a pause, run /recap to see in one sentence where the session currently stands.

whenGood.title: Share a short status update
whenGood.example: Use /recap to get a one-sentence summary of the current session state to pass along to colleagues.

whenBad.title: Saving or sharing the full conversation history
whenBad.why: /recap only gives a one-line summary, not the full text of the conversation.
whenBad.alternative: Use /export to export the entire conversation as plain text.

commands.name: /release-notes
commands.summary: Shows the changelog in an interactive version picker.
commands.what:
Shows the changelog in an interactive version picker.

A specific version can be chosen, or all versions shown.

whenGood.title: Find out what changed after an update
whenGood.example: Run /release-notes to read the changes in the most recently installed version.

whenGood.title: Look up the behavior of a specific version
whenGood.example: Call /release-notes with a specific version number to check whether a fix is included in exactly that version.

whenBad.title: Actually updating to the latest version
whenBad.why: /release-notes only displays the changelog, it doesn't perform an update itself.
whenBad.alternative: Use /upgrade to actually update.

commands.name: /reload-plugins
commands.summary: Reloads all active plugins to apply pending changes without a restart.
commands.what:
Reloads all active plugins to apply pending changes without a restart.

Reports a count per component, flags load errors. Warns and skips on prompt cache invalidation, unless --force is used.

whenGood.title: Test a change to your own plugin immediately
whenGood.example: After editing a plugin file, run /reload-plugins to apply the change without restarting the session.

whenGood.title: Catch load errors after a plugin change
whenGood.example: Use /reload-plugins to immediately get a report on whether a newly edited plugin loads without errors.

whenBad.title: Forcing a reload immediately despite a pending prompt-cache invalidation
whenBad.why: /reload-plugins skips the reload by default when it would invalidate the prompt cache.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reload-plugins --force to force the reload anyway.

whenBad.title: Only a single changed skill needs updating, not a plugin
whenBad.why: /reload-plugins reloads plugins, not project-local skills or commands.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reload-skills to re-scan skill and command directories.

commands.name: /reload-skills
commands.summary: Rescans skill and command directories so skills changed during the session become available without a restart.
commands.what:
Rescans skill and command directories so skills changed during the session become available without a restart.

Reports available/added/removed skills. Introduced in v2.1.152.

whenGood.title: Make a newly added skill available during the running session
whenGood.example: Add a skill to .claude/skills/ and then run /reload-skills instead of restarting the session.

whenGood.title: Verify a skill change was picked up correctly
whenGood.example: After editing a SKILL.md, run /reload-skills and check the report of added or removed skills.

whenBad.title: An entire plugin (not just loose skills/commands) was changed
whenBad.why: /reload-skills scans skill and command directories, not the plugin structure including loaded MCP tools.
whenBad.alternative: Use /reload-plugins to reload the entire plugin.

commands.name: /remote-control
commands.summary: Makes the session available for remote control from claude.ai.
commands.what:
Makes the session available for remote control from claude.ai.

Alias: /rc.

whenGood.title: Steer a local terminal session from a phone or the web
whenGood.example: Enable /remote-control, then steer the running terminal session from claude.ai, e.g. while away from your desk.

whenGood.title: Keep an eye on a running task from another device
whenGood.example: Turn on /remote-control before starting a longer task, so progress can be checked later from a different device.

whenBad.title: Bringing a web session into the local terminal, not the other way around
whenBad.why: /remote-control makes the local session controllable from claude.ai — it's the wrong command for the opposite direction.
whenBad.alternative: Use /teleport to bring a web session into the terminal.

whenBad.title: Simply continuing work in the desktop app on another device
whenBad.why: /remote-control makes the session remotely controllable but doesn't move it into a desktop app.
whenBad.alternative: Use /desktop to continue the session in the Claude Code desktop app (requires macOS or Windows plus a Claude subscription).

commands.name: /remote-env
commands.summary: Selects the default environment for cloud agents.
commands.what: Selects the default environment for cloud agents.

whenGood.title: Set a default environment once
whenGood.example: Before launching cloud agents from claude.ai/code repeatedly, use /remote-env to pick a default environment so you don't have to choose it every time.

whenGood.title: Switching between multiple cloud environments
whenGood.example: When moving between two repos with different cloud environments, set the new one as default with /remote-env before starting the next cloud agent.

whenBad.title: Purely local terminal session
whenBad.why: /remote-env only affects the default environment for cloud agents, not local sessions.
whenBad.alternative: No action needed — just keep working locally as normal.

commands.name: /rename
commands.summary: Renames the current session and shows the name in the prompt bar.
commands.what:
Renames the current session and shows the name in the prompt bar.

Without a name, one is generated automatically from the history. Also non-interactive (-p), requires v2.1.205+.

whenGood.title: Make a long session easy to find again
whenGood.example: Name a multi-hour debugging session with /rename login-bug-session so it's easy to spot later via /resume.

whenGood.title: Rename automatically via script
whenGood.example: In a non-interactive (-p) setup on v2.1.205+, name sessions consistently at start instead of relying on the auto-generated name.

whenBad.title: A quick throwaway question
whenBad.why: For a session that's ending with /clear right after, a custom name isn't worth the effort.
whenBad.alternative: Leave it unnamed — the auto-generated name is fine for such brief use.

commands.name: /resume
commands.summary: Resumes a conversation by ID or name, or opens the session picker.
commands.what:
Resumes a conversation by ID or name, or opens the session picker.

From v2.1.144, background sessions appear marked 'bg'. Alias: /continue.

whenGood.title: Choose between several open conversations
whenGood.example: /resume

whenGood.title: Reattach a specific background session by name
whenGood.example: /resume my-background-agent

whenBad.title: Go back to an earlier point within the current conversation
whenBad.why: /resume reopens a different, separate conversation by ID or name - it doesn't jump to an earlier point in the current session.
whenBad.alternative: /rewind

whenBad.title: Already in the right open session
whenBad.why: /resume is for switching to another conversation - without a session switch it does nothing useful.
whenBad.alternative: just keep typing, no command needed

commands.name: /review
commands.summary: Runs a quick, read-only single-pass review of a GitHub pull request by number.
commands.what:
Runs a quick, read-only single-pass review of a GitHub pull request by number.

Without an argument, a picker of open PRs appears; text after the number is an additional instruction. v2.1.186-v2.1.201 ran /review with the multi-agent engine of '/code-review medium'. Multi-agent with effort: '/code-review <level> <pr#>'; cloud: '/code-review ultra'.

whenGood.title: Quick first look at a PR
whenGood.example: Use /review 482 to quickly check before merging whether the pull request has any obvious problems, without picking an effort level.

whenGood.title: Pick a PR from a list to check
whenGood.example: Call /review with no number to choose one from the list of open PRs for a quick pass.

whenBad.title: Thorough review of a critical PR
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /review is a quick, read-only single pass — not a multi-agent review with selectable effort.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code-review <level> <pr#> for a multi-agent review with an effort level, or /code-review ultra for a deep cloud review.

whenBad.title: Pure cleanup without bug hunting
whenBad.why: /review looks for problems in the PR; it isn't built for cleanup-only passes.
whenBad.alternative: Use /simplify for reuse/simplification/efficiency without bug hunting.

commands.name: /rewind
commands.summary: Resets the conversation and/or the code to an earlier point, or summarizes from a selected message onward.
commands.what:
Resets the conversation and/or the code to an earlier point, or summarizes from a selected message onward.

Details: code.claude.com/docs/en/checkpointing. Aliases: /checkpoint, /undo.

whenGood.title: Reset conversation and code state after a fix went wrong
whenGood.example: /rewind

whenGood.title: Summarize from a selected message instead of a full reset
whenGood.example: /rewind

whenBad.title: Just want to re-read earlier text
whenBad.why: /rewind resets the conversation and potentially the code state - unnecessarily risky for the current code just to re-read something.
whenBad.alternative: scroll back up in the transcript

commands.name: /run
commands.summary: Starts and drives the project app so you can actually see a change take effect (not just tests).
commands.what:
Starts and drives the project app so you can actually see a change take effect (not just tests).

[Skill] See 'Run and verify your app' in the skills docs. Requires v2.1.145+.

whenGood.title: See a change work in the real flow
whenGood.example: After a UI change, use /run to actually start the app and see the change in the browser, instead of just trusting green tests.

whenGood.title: Check a freshly set up project
whenGood.example: On a newly cloned project, use /run to see whether the app even comes up from a clean environment.

whenBad.title: Only unit tests matter
whenBad.why: /run actively starts and drives the app — for plain test runs with no running app needed, that's unnecessary overhead.
whenBad.alternative: Run the test suite directly (e.g. npm test) without starting the app.

commands.name: /run-skill-generator
commands.summary: Teaches /run and /verify how to build, start, and drive the project app from a clean environment, by writing a project-specific skill.
commands.what:
Teaches /run and /verify how to build, start, and drive the project app from a clean environment, by writing a project-specific skill.

[Skill] Requires v2.1.145+.

whenGood.title: Set up a project skill for run/verify once
whenGood.example: On a new project, run /run-skill-generator so Claude learns how to build, start, and drive the app from a clean environment.

whenGood.title: Re-run after a build process change
whenGood.example: If the build/start process has changed fundamentally (e.g. a new build tool), run /run-skill-generator again so /run and /verify match the current setup.

whenBad.title: A working skill already exists
whenBad.why: If /run and /verify already work reliably in the project, running the generator again is redundant.
whenBad.alternative: Keep using the existing project skill, or adjust it manually if needed.

commands.name: /sandbox
commands.summary: Toggles Sandbox mode.
commands.what:
Toggles Sandbox mode.

Only available on supported platforms.

whenGood.title: Test risky commands in isolation
whenGood.example: Before running unfamiliar, generated code, enable /sandbox to run it in isolation (on supported platforms).

whenGood.title: Turn sandbox back off after the task
whenGood.example: After finishing the risky step, call /sandbox again to switch back to normal mode.

whenBad.title: Unsupported platform
whenBad.why: Per the docs, sandbox mode is only available on supported platforms.
whenBad.alternative: Check the sandboxing docs for whether your platform is supported before expecting the command to work.

commands.name: /schedule
commands.summary: Creates, updates, lists, or runs routines that run on a schedule on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure.
commands.what:
Creates, updates, lists, or runs routines that run on a schedule on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure.

Claude walks you through the setup conversationally. Alias: /routines.

whenGood.title: A recurring check that survives ending the session
whenGood.example: Use /schedule to set up a daily routine that runs on Anthropic-managed cloud infrastructure — it keeps running even after the machine or session is long closed.

whenGood.title: A one-time future task independent of the current session
whenGood.example: Use /schedule to set up a one-off future task that doesn't depend on the current session staying open.

whenBad.title: Quick polling within the current session
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /loop is meant for quick polling during an open session (session-scoped, ends with the session); /schedule is for routines on cloud infrastructure with a minimum interval of 1 hour.
whenBad.alternative: Use /loop 5m ... as long as the session stays open anyway and the interval is under an hour.

whenBad.title: The task needs access to local files
whenBad.why: Per the docs, cloud routines run on a fresh clone with no access to local files.
whenBad.alternative: Use a Desktop scheduled task or /loop, both of which have local file access.

commands.name: /scroll-speed
commands.summary: Interactively adjusts the mouse wheel scroll speed.
commands.what:
Interactively adjusts the mouse wheel scroll speed.

Full-screen rendering only, not in the JetBrains IDE terminal.

whenGood.title: Mouse wheel scrolling feels off
whenGood.example: In the fullscreen terminal, call /scroll-speed and adjust the mouse wheel speed live against the ruler preview.

whenBad.title: JetBrains IDE terminal or non-fullscreen
whenBad.why: Per the docs, only available in fullscreen rendering, not in the JetBrains IDE terminal.
whenBad.alternative: Use the IDE's own terminal scroll settings.

commands.name: /security-review
commands.summary: Analyzes pending changes on the current branch for security vulnerabilities.
commands.what:
Analyzes pending changes on the current branch for security vulnerabilities.

Checks the git diff for risks like injection, auth issues, and data leaks.

whenGood.title: Before merging an auth/secrets feature
whenGood.example: Before merging a branch that adds a new API endpoint handling user input, run /security-review to check the diff for injection and auth issues.

whenGood.title: After adding payment/sensitive feature code
whenGood.example: After payment logic or secret handling has been added to the current branch, run /security-review specifically before opening the PR.

whenBad.title: General code quality without a security focus
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /security-review specifically checks for security risks like injection, auth issues, and data leaks — not general cleanup.
whenBad.alternative: Use /simplify or /code-review for general quality/bugs.

commands.name: /setup-bedrock
commands.summary: Configures Amazon Bedrock authentication, region, and model pins via an interactive wizard.
commands.what:
Configures Amazon Bedrock authentication, region, and model pins via an interactive wizard.

Only visible with CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1.

whenGood.title: Team is switching to Amazon Bedrock
whenGood.example: With CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 set, run /setup-bedrock to interactively configure authentication, region, and model pins.

whenGood.title: Update model pins after a Bedrock change
whenGood.example: After a change to available Bedrock models, run /setup-bedrock again to re-pin them.

whenBad.title: Without the env var set
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /setup-bedrock is only visible when CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK=1 is set.
whenBad.alternative: Set the env var first, or just use /login for normal Anthropic account usage.

commands.name: /setup-vertex
commands.summary: Configures authentication, project, region, and model pins for Google Cloud's Agent Platform.
commands.what:
Configures authentication, project, region, and model pins for Google Cloud's Agent Platform.

Only visible with CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1.

whenGood.title: Team uses Google Cloud's Agent Platform
whenGood.example: With CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1 set, run /setup-vertex to configure authentication, project, region, and model pins.

whenGood.title: Project or region change on Vertex
whenGood.example: After switching Google Cloud projects, run /setup-vertex again to adjust the configuration.

whenBad.title: Without the env var set
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /setup-vertex is only visible when CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX=1 is set.
whenBad.alternative: Set the env var first, or use /login for normal Anthropic account usage.

commands.name: /simplify
commands.summary: Reviews changed code for cleanup opportunities (reuse, simplification, efficiency) and applies the fixes directly, without hunting for correctness bugs.
commands.what:
Reviews changed code for cleanup opportunities (reuse, simplification, efficiency) and applies the fixes directly, without hunting for correctness bugs.

[Skill] Four review agents run in parallel: reuse, simplification, efficiency, abstraction level. From v2.1.154, no bug hunting (use /code-review for that). A path or PR reference can be given as the target.

whenGood.title: Clean up after a finished feature
whenGood.example: Once a feature already works and is tested, run /simplify on the changed code to improve reuse, simplification, and efficiency.

whenGood.title: Clean up one path without bug hunting
whenGood.example: Call /simplify src/utils/parser.ts to clean up just that file without also hunting for correctness bugs.

whenBad.title: Bugs need to be found too
whenBad.why: Per the docs, since v2.1.154 /simplify explicitly no longer hunts for correctness bugs, only cleanup potential.
whenBad.alternative: Use /code-review, which also covers correctness bugs.

commands.name: /skills
commands.summary: Lists available skills.
commands.what:
Lists available skills.

From v2.1.121, filterable by typing. 't' sorts by token count. Space toggles visibility, Enter saves.

whenGood.title: Overview of loaded skills
whenGood.example: Call /skills and sort by token count with 't' to see which skills cost the most context.

whenGood.title: Hide unused skills
whenGood.example: In the /skills list, toggle individual skills invisible with spacebar to save context, then save with Enter.

whenBad.title: Diagnosing broken skill installs
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /skills just lists available skills without diagnosing installation problems.
whenBad.alternative: Use /doctor, which checks installation, duplicate/broken skills, and context costs.

commands.name: /stats
commands.summary: Alias for /usage, opens directly to the Stats tab.
commands.what: Alias for /usage, opens directly to the Stats tab.

whenGood.title: Quick cost check
whenGood.example: Call /stats to jump straight to the Stats tab and see token and cost usage without navigating through /usage first.

whenBad.title: Referencing it as a distinct command in docs/scripts
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /stats is just an alias for /usage that opens directly on the Stats tab — no separate functionality.
whenBad.alternative: Reference /usage instead, which covers the same functionality.

commands.name: /status
commands.summary: Opens the settings interface to the Status tab with version, model, account, and connectivity.
commands.what:
Opens the settings interface to the Status tab with version, model, account, and connectivity.

Also works while Claude is responding.

whenGood.title: Check version/model/account
whenGood.example: Call /status to see which Claude Code version, model, and account are currently active, e.g. when debugging unexpected behavior.

whenGood.title: Check connectivity mid-response
whenGood.example: While Claude is still responding, open /status to check connectivity without interrupting the current answer.

whenBad.title: Looking for token/cost info
whenBad.why: /status shows version, model, account, and connectivity, not usage/cost data.
whenBad.alternative: Use /usage.

commands.name: /statusline
commands.summary: Configures Claude Code's status line.
commands.what:
Configures Claude Code's status line.

Describe what you want, or leave it without an argument for auto-configuration from the shell prompt.

whenGood.title: Show custom info in the status line
whenGood.example: Describe what you want to /statusline, e.g. 'show me the current git branch and token cost', to configure the status line accordingly.

whenGood.title: Derive it automatically from the shell prompt
whenGood.example: Call /statusline with no argument so it derives the configuration automatically from your existing shell prompt.

whenBad.title: Just changing the prompt bar's color
whenBad.why: /statusline configures the status line's content/layout, not its color.
whenBad.alternative: Use /color to just set the color.

commands.name: /stickers
commands.summary: Orders Claude Code stickers.
commands.what: Orders Claude Code stickers.

whenGood.title: Order merch for yourself or the team
whenGood.example: Call /stickers to order Claude Code stickers.

whenBad.title: Expecting a dev benefit
whenBad.why: /stickers has no bearing on actual development work — it's purely merch ordering.
whenBad.alternative: Use one of the development commands for productive work, not /stickers.

commands.name: /tasks
commands.summary: Lists background tasks of the current session and attached background sessions.
commands.what: Lists background tasks of the current session and attached background sessions.

whenGood.title: Check running background subagents
whenGood.example: After starting several subagents with /batch, call /tasks to see what's still open across the current session and any attached background sessions.

whenGood.title: Get an overview before ending the session
whenGood.example: Before /exit, quickly check /tasks to see if any background tasks are still running that you don't want to lose.

whenBad.title: Viewing scheduled cron/loop schedules
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /tasks lists the session's background tasks, not the schedules created via /loop or /schedule.
whenBad.alternative: Ask Claude directly ('what scheduled tasks do I have?') for /loop schedules, or use /schedule for cloud routines.

commands.name: /teleport
commands.summary: Brings a web session into the terminal.
commands.what:
Brings a web session into the terminal.

/remote-control does the reverse, letting you continue the local session from another device.

whenGood.title: Continue a web session locally
whenGood.example: Pull a session started on claude.ai/code into the local terminal with /teleport to keep working with local file access.

whenGood.title: Continue on a more capable local machine
whenGood.example: Pull a task started on the phone via claude.ai/code onto your workstation terminal with /teleport to continue with full tool access.

whenBad.title: The reverse case: continue a local session on the go
whenBad.why: /teleport pulls a web session into the terminal, not the other way around.
whenBad.alternative: Use /remote-control to control the local session remotely from another device.

commands.name: /thinking
commands.summary: Turns Extended Thinking on or off for the current session.
commands.what:
Turns Extended Thinking on or off for the current session.

Without an argument, a picker with thinking effort levels appears. Takes effect immediately from the next response. From v2.1.205 also non-interactive (-p).

whenGood.title: Let Claude think harder on a tricky problem
whenGood.example: Before a complex architecture decision, turn on /thinking so Claude reasons more extensively before answering.

whenGood.title: Turn it off again for simple requests
whenGood.example: After the tricky part is done, turn /thinking off again to get snappier answers on simple follow-up questions.

whenBad.title: Controlling overall effort across multiple turns
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /thinking only toggles extended thinking before the next answer, not the general effort level (tool use, thoroughness).
whenBad.alternative: Use /effort to set the effort level (low through max/ultracode).

commands.name: /upgrade
commands.summary: Updates Claude Code to the latest version.
commands.what:
Updates Claude Code to the latest version.

Only visible on Pro and Max plans.

whenGood.title: Update to the latest version
whenGood.example: Call /upgrade as soon as a new Claude Code version is available to install it directly.

whenBad.title: Without a Pro/Max plan
whenBad.why: Per the docs, /upgrade is only visible on Pro and Max plans.
whenBad.alternative: Update via the regular install path/package manager instead.

commands.name: /usage
commands.summary: Shows API usage, token counts, and spending.
commands.what:
Shows API usage, token counts, and spending.

Aliases: /cost, /stats.

whenGood.title: Regular cost overview
whenGood.example: Call /usage to keep an eye on token counts and spend before starting a bigger task.

whenGood.title: Trace the cost of an expensive task
whenGood.example: After a long /batch run or agent loop, check /usage to see what it cost.

whenBad.title: Checking the session's context window usage
whenBad.why: /usage shows API usage/cost, not how full the current context window is.
